                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Type Hints & Static Analysis
  1.3  Project Structure & Virtual Environments
  1.4  Documentation & Docstrings
  1.5  Error Handling & Logging
  1.6  Performance & Idiomatic Python

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Third-Party, Most Popular)
  2.4  nose2 (Successor to nose)
  2.5  Hypothesis (Property-Based Testing)
  2.6  Framework Comparison Table

PART 3: WRITING EFFECTIVE TESTS
  3.1  Test Structure & Naming
  3.2  Fixtures, Mocks & Parametrization
  3.3  Code Coverage
  3.4  Continuous Integration Tips

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Follow PEP 8, the official Python style guide.
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters (docstrings/comments) or 99 characters (code).
- Use blank lines to separate functions, classes, and logical sections:
    - 2 blank lines before top-level functions/classes.
    - 1 blank line before methods inside a class.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Third-party imports
    3. Local application imports
- Use snake_case for variables, functions, and methods.
- Use PascalCase for class names.
- Use UPPER_CASE for constants.
- Avoid extraneous whitespace inside parentheses, brackets, or braces.

Tools to enforce style:
  - black   : Opinionated auto-formatter (run: black .)
  - flake8  : Linter that checks PEP 8 compliance
  - ruff    : Fast Python linter and formatter (modern alternative)
  - isort   : Automatically sorts imports

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and catch bugs early.
- Annotate function signatures and complex variables:

    def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str:
        return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."

- Use Optional[X] instead of Union[X, None] (Python 3.10+ use X | None).
- Use mypy or pyright for static type checking.
- Leverage TypedDict, Protocol, and dataclasses for structured data.

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE & VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS
----------------------------------------------
A well-organized project structure:

    my_project/
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module.py
    │       └── utils.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module.py
    │   └── conftest.py
    ├── pyproject.toml       # Modern build config (or setup.cfg)
    ├── requirements.txt     # Pinned dependencies
    ├── README.md
    └── .gitignore

- Always use virtual environments (venv, virtualenv, or conda).
- Pin dependencies with requirements.txt or use Poetry/Pipenv for lock files.
- Use pyproject.toml for project metadata, build system, and tool config.
- Keep configuration in one place (pyproject.toml) when possible.

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & DOCSTRINGS
-------------------------------
- Use docstrings for all public modules, classes, and functions (PEP 257).
- Prefer Google-style or NumPy-style docstrings for readability:

    def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
        """Add two integers and return the result.

        Args:
            a: The first integer.
            b: The second integer.

        Returns:
            The sum of a and b.

        Rais

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Follow PEP 8, the official Python style guide.
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters (docstrings/comments) or 99 characters (code).
- Use blank lines to separate functions, classes, and logical sections:
    - 2 blank lines before top-level functions/classes.
    - 1 blank line before methods inside a class.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Third-party imports
    3. Local application imports
- Use snake_case for variables, functions, and methods.
- Use PascalCase for class names.
- Use UPPER_CASE for constants.
- Avoid extraneous whitespace inside parentheses, brackets, or braces.

Tools to enforce style:
  - black   : Opinionated auto-formatter (run: black .)
  - flake8  : Linter that checks PEP 8 compliance
  - ruff    : Fast Python linter and formatter (modern alternative)
  - isort   : Automatically sorts imports

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and catch bugs early.
- Annotate function signatures and complex variables:

    def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str:
        return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."

- Use Optional[X] or X | None (Python 3.10+) for nullable types.
- Use mypy or pyright for static type checking.
- Leverage TypedDict, Protocol, and dataclasses for structured data.

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE & VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS
----------------------------------------------
A well-organized project structure:

    my_project/
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module.py
    │       └── utils.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module.py
    │   └── conftest.py
    ├── pyproject.toml       # Modern build config
    ├── requirements.txt     # Pinned dependencies
    ├── README.md
    └── .gitignore

- Always use virtual environments (venv, virtualenv, or conda).
- Pin dependencies with requirements.txt or use Poetry/Pipenv for lock files.
- Use pyproject.toml for project metadata, build system, and tool config.
- Keep configuration in one place (pyproject.toml) when possible.

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & DOCSTRINGS
-------------------------------
- Use docstrings for all public modules, classes, and functions (PEP 257).
- Prefer Google-style or NumPy-style docstrings:

    def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
        """Add two integers and return the result.

        Args:
            a: The first integer.
            b: The second integer.

        Returns:
            The sum of a and b.

        Raises:
            TypeError: If either argument is not an integer.
        """
        if not isinstance(a, int) or not isinstance(b, int):
            raise TypeError("Both arguments must be integers.")
